notes: MADlib training is a procedure-style call; prediction is a scalar function applied per row

fbsql: relation as SQL string + R formula string + named arguments
madlib: source table NAME as string + dependent column name + independent variables as an SQL array expression string ('ARRAY[1; age; ...]')
notes: MADlib does not accept an inline subquery; encoded tables must be materialized first

fbsql: model relation (SQL string) + scoring relation (SQL string)
madlib: coefficient array column + hand-built feature array per row (manual CROSS JOIN with the model table)
notes: the caller must repeat the exact feature construction used at fit time

fbsql: single relation; one row per term with a named term column + metadata jsonb
madlib: two tables created by side effect: <out> (one row per model; parallel arrays coef/std_err/z_stats/p_values) + <out>_summary (call parameters as strings)

fbsql: term-named rows directly readable in SQL
madlib: positional arrays; the term-to-position mapping exists only at the call site
notes: unnest with a hand-written term array was needed to produce a readable table

fbsql: automatic (glm conventions: sorted levels; treatment contrasts; xlevels stored)
madlib: manual one-hot encoding by the user before training (reference level dropped by hand); encode_categorical_variables helper exists but is a separate preprocessing step

fbsql: Complete Case Analysis; n_obs / n_used / n_dropped columns in the output relation
madlib: rows with NULL skipped; num_rows_processed / num_missing_rows_skipped columns in the model table
notes: both are complete-case; FbSQL reports it per fit relation while MADlib reports it in the model table

madlib: NULL array element yields NULL prediction (observed: c104)
undetectable by design: manual dummies are all zero so unseen levels are silently scored as the reference level (observed: c105 -> 0.2020)
notes: key safety difference; MADlib has no fit-time level information to check against

fbsql: yes (any SQL string defining a relation)
yes (one relation from a set-returning function)
madlib: no for fit (void return; writes two tables by side effect); predict returns a scalar per row
positional string arguments in documented usage
notes: MADlib functions are ordinary PostgreSQL functions so => is possible in principle but not the documented style

fbsql: metadata jsonb column (xlevels / contrasts / terms; SQL-inspectable)
madlib: <out>_summary table stores the call strings (source_table / dependent_varname / independent_varname / optimizer params)
notes: MADlib stores the expression text rather than structured model metadata such as factor levels

madlib: coefficients match FbSQL/R to 4 decimals; one std_err differs in the 4th decimal (IRLS tolerance)
